perturbation.original_text: Any notice to be given under this Agr eement shall be in writing and sen t by facsimile transmission or s ent by re corded delivery post to the receiving party at its business address as last not ified in writing to the other party and shall be deemed to have been given on the date of the facsimile transmission or two days following that on w hich the notice was posted.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice to be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and sent by standard postal service to the receiving party at its business address as last notified in writing to the other party and shall be deemed to have been given five days following that on which the notice was posted, regardless of actual delivery date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification clause to only allow standard post and deeming it received in 5 days creates a potential issue if the receiving party does not receive the letter within that timeframe, conflicting with legal requirements for adequate notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification mandating postal service and a fixed 5-day deeming provision may contradict Section 13(2) of the Electronic Transactions Act 1999 (Cth) because excluding electronic methods and applying a fixed deemed receipt time may not align with proving actual receipt or allowing parties to agree otherwise.

perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that notice is deemed to be given five days after posting, regardless of the actual delivery date, and exclusively via standard postal service. The Law Explanation suggests this conflicts with Section 13(2) of the Electronic Transactions Act 1999 (Cth) because it excludes electronic methods and applies a fixed deemed receipt time which might not align with proving actual receipt. While the scraped snippets provide no direct support, the Law Explanation coupled with the Perturbation Explanation, clearly suggests that the fixed timeframe and method restriction create a contradiction by potentially undermining legally adequate notice.

perturbation.original_text: This agree ment will b e effective as of the date below, and contin ue for five (5) years.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement will be effective as of the date below, and continue indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Removing a fixed term (5 years) can create legal uncertainty and issues with enforceability, especially considering the nature of confidential information which might become obsolete or less sensitive over time. This also imposes an indefinite restriction on EFCA Pty Ltd.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4(1) of the Restraints of Trade Act 1976 (VIC) states that a restraint of trade is valid to the extent that it is not against public policy. An indefinite confidentiality agreement could be seen as against public policy if it unreasonably restricts EFCA Pty Ltd's ability to operate in the future, especially if the confidential information becomes outdated or irrelevant, meaning it could be deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: BarNet Jade - Find recent Australian legal decisions, judgments, case summaries for legal professionals (Judgments And Decisions Enhanced)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes a fixed-term agreement (5 years) to an indefinite one. The Law Explanation suggests this indefinite term could violate Section 4(1) of the Restraints of Trade Act 1976 (VIC) if it unreasonably restricts EFCA Pty Ltd's future operations, especially if the confidential information becomes outdated. The original text with a fixed term provides a clear boundary, while the changed text introduces uncertainty, creating a contradiction by potentially violating the principle that restraints of trade must be reasonable and not against public policy.

perturbation.original_text: EFCA agrees to keep the existenc e and nature of this Agreement confidential and not to use the same or the name of the disclos ing party (or of any company in the Group of Companies of which the disclosing party forms part) in any public ity, adv ertisement or other disclosure with regard to this Agreement without the pr ior written consent of the disclos ing party.
perturbation.changed_text: EFCA agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreement confidential and not to disclose the same or the name of the disclosing party (or of any company in the Group of Companies of which the disclosing party forms part) in any manner, including in response to a subpoena, court order, or other legal process, without the prior written consent of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Forbidding disclosure even in response to a legal subpoena or court order directly contradicts legal obligations to comply with such orders.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 32 of the Evidence Act 2008 (VIC) compels individuals to provide evidence when legally required. The modified text prevents EFCA from disclosing information even when compelled by a subpoena or court order, directly contradicting the legal obligation to provide evidence, potentially leading to penalties for contempt of court.

perturbation.justification: The modified text prevents EFCA from disclosing information even when legally required by a subpoena or court order. This directly contradicts the legal obligation to provide evidence when compelled by law. The Law Explanation states that Section 32 of the Evidence Act 2008 (VIC) compels individuals to provide evidence, which is undermined by the changed contract text. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the Evidence Act 2008 is in force. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that forbidding disclosure in response to a legal subpoena contradicts legal obligations to comply with such orders.

perturbation.original_text: Copies or reproductions shall not be made exc ept to the ext ent reasonably necessary and all co pies made shall be the property of the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: Copies or reproductions shall not be made under any circumstances, and all copies made will be immediately destroyed without archiving.
perturbation.explanation: Preventing copies under all circumstances, and requiring immediate destruction could conflict with mandatory record-keeping requirements depending on the nature of the 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 286 of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) requires companies to keep financial records that correctly record and explain its transactions and financial position and performance and would enable true and fair financial statements to be prepared and audited. If any of the Confidential Information related to EFCA's financial transactions, then a blanket ban on making or keeping copies would contravene this legal obligation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction. The original text allows copies or reproductions to the extent reasonably necessary. The changed text prohibits copies under any circumstances and requires immediate destruction of all copies without archiving. The law explanation states that Section 286 of the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) requires companies to keep financial records. Because the changed text prohibits copies of 'Confidential Information' under any circumstances, the modified contract directly contradicts the legal obligation to maintain financial records.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the 2 foregoing, EFCA shall be entitled to make any disc losure required by law of t he disc losing party’s Confide ntial Information provide d that it gives the disc losing party not less t han two business days’ notice of such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, EFCA shall be entitled to make any disclosure required by law of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information provided that it gives the disclosing party not more than one hour’s notice of such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only a one-hour notice before legally required disclosure may be insufficient for the disclosing party to seek legal remedies to protect the confidential information, particularly if the disclosing party is located in a different time zone, or the legal requirement arises unexpectedly.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 14.05 of the Civil Procedure Rules 2010 (VIC) mandates reasonable notice. While it doesn't specify a number of days, one-hour’s notice is unlikely to be considered 'reasonable' in most cases, particularly if the disclosure involves sensitive information where the disclosing party might need to seek urgent legal advice or an injunction to protect their interests.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided 'not less than two business days’ notice' for legally required disclosures, while the changed contract reduces this to 'not more than one hour’s notice.' The Law Explanation states that Rule 14.05 of the Civil Procedure Rules 2010 (VIC) mandates 'reasonable notice' and that 'one-hour’s notice is unlikely to be considered 'reasonable' in most cases.' This change introduces a time constraint that is likely to be deemed unreasonable, thus creating a contradiction with the implicitly required 'reasonable notice' as interpreted by the Law Explanation. The shortened time frame significantly hinders the disclosing party's ability to seek legal recourse, undermining the intention of reasonable notice. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal expectations.
